title: Cutting a dovetail joint by hand
subtitle: The foundational technique for the through-dovetail in hardwood
excerpt: How to cut a through-dovetail joint by hand: marking out tails and pins, sawing and chiselling to the line, fitting tails to pins until the joint pulls together cleanly, and understanding what a good dovetail looks like before glue.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Carpentry for Boys (1914). The Mechanic's Workshop Companion.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fan-shaped projections cut on one piece of the dovetail joint, so named because they resemble a dove's tail in profile. The tails are cut first in hand-cut dovetail work; they are then used to scribe the pin positions directly onto the adjacent piece.

The narrow projections on the mating piece of a dovetail joint that fit between the tails. The pin positions are scribed directly from the cut tails so any error in the tail layout transfers to the pins exactly, keeping the fit tight.

The line marked across the face of the timber at the shoulder depth of the dovetail joint. No saw cut goes below this line on either piece. The baseline on the pins piece must equal the board thickness of the tails piece, and vice versa.

content.text: The hand-cut dovetail is the most mechanically sound corner joint in cabinet making. The interlocking shape of
tails
and
pins

resists racking and pull in one direction with or without glue. The technique requires attention and practice rather than exceptional physical skill: the marks tell you exactly where to cut, and the chisel work simply follows the marks.
When to use it

Use through-dovetails at the corners of boxes, drawers, carcasses, and chests. The joint is visible from both sides in the finished piece; it is both structural and decorative. Half-blind dovetails (not covered here) conceal the joint from the front face and are used in drawer fronts where the tails must not show.
What you will need

Two pieces of dry hardwood (oak, ash, walnut, or beech) planed to finished thickness. A tenon saw with a clean kerf (15 TPI is ideal). A 6 mm and a 12 mm bevel-edge chisel, sharp. A wooden mallet. A marking gauge, a sliding bevel set to the dovetail angle (1:6 for softwood, 1:8 for hardwood), a marking knife, and a combination square.
Step 1, set out the baseline
Set the marking gauge to the thickness of the adjacent board. Score the
baseline
across the face and both edges of each piece. This line is the limit of every cut; no saw or chisel goes below it.
Step 2, mark and cut the tails

Mark the tail positions on the tail piece end-grain using a pencil: one half-pin at each edge, equal-width tails between them. Set the sliding bevel to 1:8 and mark the angle of each tail on the face of the board from the pencilled spacing. Score the angled lines with the marking knife and carry them square across the end grain. Saw down each line on the waste side, stopping exactly at the baseline. Chop out the waste between tails with the chisel, working halfway through from each face.
Step 3, mark the pins from the tails

Hold the tails piece square and flush over the end of the pins piece and scribe around the tails with the marking knife. Every mark made by the knife is a direct transfer from the cut joint; there is no measurement to make here. Carry each scribed line square down the face of the pins piece to the baseline.
Step 4, cut the pins and fit

Saw down the pin lines on the waste side, stopping at the baseline. Chop the waste with the chisel. Offer the joint together: it should pull together under hand pressure with no hammer required. If it resists, look for the shiny mark left on the surface of the tails by the tight pin, and pare that spot with the chisel. Good fit requires repeated offer-and-pare, not a single pass.
What it looks like when it is right

A correctly fitted dovetail joint pulls together under hand pressure and has no visible gaps at the baseline on either piece. Light at the corners of the joint is acceptable; light at the middle of a long glue surface is not. The joint should require a moderate tap with the mallet to pull home fully; if it requires heavy hammer blows, it will split the timber on assembly.
Common mistakes
Sawing too fast and drifting off the line. The tenon saw cuts faster than many beginners expect. Establish the kerf with two or three slow push strokes before cutting freely.
Chiselling past the baseline. Set a second marking gauge at the baseline and use it as a visual stop. Any cut that crosses the baseline leaves a visible gap in the finished joint.
Scribing pins from poorly cut tails. Ugly tails produce ugly pins; cut tails as accurately as possible before scribing. The pins inherit any error in the tails exactly.
